title: Crack a Leaked Unsalted MD5 Hash
objective: Recover the admin password from a leaked database dump by cracking its unsalted MD5 hash.
briefing: A leaked dump.sql exposes a users table with a password_md5 column - no salt, no per-user variation. The {victim_username} row's hash is 5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99.
hints: Unsalted MD5 means identical passwords always produce this exact hash - it may already be a known one.
Look this hash up against a list of common leaked passwords.
debrief: Because the password was hashed with plain MD5 and no per-user salt, every account using the same password produces the identical hash - and that exact hash is one of the most common ones ever leaked online, so it cracks instantly against any common-password wordlist. This is a Cryptographic Failure (OWASP A02:2021): using a fast, unsalted, general-purpose hash for passwords instead of a slow, salted, password-specific one. The fix is bcrypt/argon2/scrypt with a unique salt per user, which makes both dictionary lookups and brute force dramatically slower and defeats identical-hash comparison across users.

logs_append: [CRYPTO] {victim_username} MD5 hash cracked via wordlist - plaintext recovered
